slides.name: Aravind Govindhasamy
slides.title: Enterprise Technical Architect | OSS Advocate
slides.oss_experience: Lead Contributor to Bude Global Tech Frameworks

box.title: 🚀 Why Microservices Now?
box.content:
🏗️ Beyond Simple Decomposition

In 2026, microservices are no longer just about splitting a monolith. It is about Organizational Scalability, Technological Diversity, and Fault Isolation. As businesses demand faster time-to-market, the ability to deploy individual capabilities independently becomes the ultimate competitive advantage.

However, with great power comes great complexity—distributed transactions, network reliability, and data consistency are the new challenges every Architect must master.

note.text: Start by setting the stage for why we moved from Monoliths to Microservices and what the current challenges are.

slides.title: Monolith vs Microservices in 2026
leftPoints: ⚡ Zero network latency for inter-module calls.
📉 Scaling requires duplicating the entire app.
🏗️ Best for: Initial MVP, small teams, low complexity.
rightPoints: 🎯 Tech stack variety (Nest, Go, Rust per service).
🛡️ Fault isolation - one crash doesn't stop the system.
📈 Handles extreme load and massive dev teams.
🏗️ Best for: Enterprise scale, complex domains.

note.text: Use this slide to emphasize that Microservices are not always the answer—it's a trade-off.

slides.question: What is the primary trade-off when moving from a Monolith to Microservices?
slides.explanation: Microservices introduce distributed system challenges like network latency, partial failures, and data consistency issues, drastically increasing complexity.

slides.title: 1. Solid Grounding
slides.subtitle: The philosophy and under-the-hood core concepts of NestJS.

slides.title: The Power of TypeScript & Nest
slides.content:
📜 Type-Safe Architecture

NestJS brings the power of Enterprise Design Patterns to Node.js. Built with TypeScript, it provides native support for decorators, interfaces, and metadata reflection. This allows for a declarative programming style that reduces boilerplate and increases readability.

By leveraging Express or Fastify as the underlying engine, Nest gives you the speed of Node with the structure of Angular or Spring Boot.
slides.caption: NestJS: The Angular of the Backend

box.title: Inversion of Control (IoC)
box.content: Nest's IoC container is the heart of the framework. It manages the lifecycle and instantiation of your providers. Understanding Scopes is critical: DEFAULT (singleton), REQUEST (per-request instance), and TRANSIENT (new instance every time).

note.text: Explain that DI makes the code highly testable by allowing easy mocking of dependencies.

slides.title: The Modular System Architecture

box.title: Encapsulation & Reusability

slides.content:
🏗️ Domain Bounded Contexts

Modules in NestJS are not just organizational tools; they are the implementation of Bounded Contexts from DDD. Each module should encapsulate related logic, models, and providers, exporting only what is necessary for the rest of the application.

This modularity is what enables the easy transition from a Monolith to Microservices later.

slides.question: Which DI Scope creates a new instance of a provider for every incoming request?
slides.explanation: Scope.REQUEST ensures that the provider is instantiated once per request lifecycle, allowing it to store request-specific data like auth tokens or logging IDs.

slides.title: Custom Decorators & Metadata
slides.content:
✨ Meta-Programming with Nest

Decorators allow you to attach metadata to classes, methods, and parameters. This metadata is then used by Guards, Interceptors, and Pipes to modify behavior at runtime without cluttering your business logic.

Using `Reflector`, you can create sophisticated logic that reacts to these decorators throughout your application pipeline.

slides.title: 2. Inside the Box
slides.subtitle: Mastering Layered Architecture and Hexagonal Design.

note.text: Hexagonal architecture decouples business logic from external dependencies like databases or APIs.

box.title: Handling HTTP & RPC
box.content:
🎯 Responsibility: Orchestration only

The Controller should be THIN. Its only jobs are: payload validation (via Pipes), security (via Guards), and delegating to the Application Service. Never put business logic here.

box.title: The Core Logic
box.content:
🧠 Domain-Driven Logic

Entities should be more than just 'Anemic Models' with getters/setters. They should contain self-validation and business rules. For example, an Order entity should have a calculateTotal() method that encapsulates the tax and discount logic.

Domain Services handle logic that doesn't naturally fit into a single entity, such as coordinating multiple domain models for a single task.

slides.question: In Hexagonal Architecture, what is the role of the 'Port'?
options: A technical interface used to communicate with external tools
slides.explanation: Ports represent the interfaces (contracts) that define how the Domain interacts with the outside world, while Adapters are the concrete implementations.

slides.title: 3. Distributed Power
slides.subtitle: Choosing the right communication protocol for your ecosystem.

slides.title: Transport Layers Overview
slides.content:
📡 Connectivity Styles

NestJS Microservices can communicate via multiple protocols. Choosing the correct one is a foundational decision. TCP/IP is the default but lacks a broker. Redis uses Pub/Sub but lacks persistence. gRPC is king for performance, while Kafka and RabbitMQ are the gold standards for reliable messaging.

box.title: Protobuf & Dynamic Clients
box.content:
⚡ High Performance RPC

gRPC (Google Remote Procedure Call) uses Protocol Buffers for serialization, which is significantly faster and smaller than JSON. NestJS provides a seamless decorator-based implementation where you can map Proto services to class methods easily.

box.title: Exchanges & Queues
box.content:
🐇 Reliable Messaging

RabbitMQ is a sophisticated message broker that supports complex routing patterns: Direct, Fanout, Topic, and Headers. Use it when you need guaranteed message delivery and durability but don't need the massive throughput of Kafka.

Key concept: Acknowledgements ensure that if a worker crashes, the message is returned to the queue.

box.title: The Unified Log
box.content:
📊 High-Throughput Streams

Kafka is more than a message broker; it's a distributed commit log. Use it for Event Sourcing or when your system needs to handle millions of events per second. Kafka allows you to 'replay' events, making it indispensable for data analytics and auditing.

box.title: Database vs Message Broker
box.content:
⚠️ The Atomic Nightmare

What happens if you update your database but the message broker is down? Or vice versa? You end up with inconsistent state. This is exactly why Transactional Outbox Pattern is essential for any production microservice.

slides.title: Transactional Outbox Pattern
slides.content:
🛡️ Atomic Consistency

Instead of publishing a message directly, save it in an Outbox table in the same database transaction as your business logic. A separate 'Messenger' process then polls the table and publishes messages to the broker. This ensures 100% reliable delivery.
slides.caption: Reliable Messaging workflow

note.text: Sagas manage long-running transactions spanning multiple services.

slides.title: Saga Orchestration vs Choreography
leftPoints: ✅ Simple to start, no single orchestrator.
❌ Hard to maintain and visualize flow.
rightPoints: ❌ Risk of single point of failure.

box.title: Undoing Errors
box.content:
🔄 Eventual Consistency

In the world of Sagas, there are no 'rollbacks' in the traditional sense. Instead, we use Compensating Transactions. If Step 2 fails, we must trigger an 'Undo' for Step 1. For example, if Payment fails, we must trigger RESTORE_INVENTORY.

slides.title: What is CQRS?
slides.content:
📡 Commands vs Queries

Command Query Responsibility Segregation separates read and write operations. The write side (Commands) focuses on business rules and state changes, while the read side (Queries) is optimized for UI presentation, often using different data models entirely.

box.title: @CommandHandler()
box.content: Commands are instructions to change state. They are handled by specialized classes that encapsulate the execution logic. This keeps your services clean and focused on orchestration.

box.title: Reading with Performance
box.content:
📊 Read Side Excellence

On the query side, you don't need business logic—you need SPEED. You can bypass your ORM, use raw SQL, or even read from a denormalized database like MongoDB or Elasticsearch. The Query Bus facilitates this separation perfectly.

slides.title: The NestJS CQRS Lifecycle
slides.content:
Client ──► Controller ──► Command Bus ──► Command Handler
                                              │
                                              ▼
                                        Domain Entity ──► Database
                                              │
                                              ▼
   Read Client ◄── Query Resolver ◄── Query Bus ◄── Query Handler

note.text: CQRS is often combined with Event Sourcing for ultimate scalability.

slides.question: What is the primary benefit of separating Queries from Commands in CQRS?
options: Independent scaling and optimization of Read/Write operations
slides.explanation: CQRS allows you to optimize your read side for complex UI queries without impacting the write side's data integrity rules.

slides.title: 6. The Unbreakable System
slides.subtitle: Circuit Breakers, Retries, and Rate Limiting.

box.title: Preventing Cascading Failures
box.content:
🔌 Stop the Bleeding

When a downstream service is down, don't keep hammering it—you'll exhaust your own resources. Use a Circuit Breaker. If failure threshold is reached, the circuit OPENS, and all requests fail immediately (or use a fallback). After some time, it goes to HALF-OPEN to test the waters.

slides.title: Retry with Exponential Backoff
slides.content:
⏳ Smart Retries

Don't just retry every second. Use Exponential Backoff with Jitter. This prevents a 'thundering herd' problem where all your services retry at the exact same moment, causing a secondary DDOS on your infrastructure.
slides.caption: Resilience in Distributed Systems

box.title: Protecting the Perimeter
box.content:
🛡️ Resource Preservation

Use @nestjs/throttler to protect your system from abuse. In a microservices environment, use a centralized store (like Redis) for your throttler state to ensure limits are enforced across multiple instances of your service.

slides.question: What happens when a Circuit Breaker is in the 'OPEN' state?
options: It immediately fails all incoming requests without calling the target.
slides.explanation: In 'OPEN' state, the breaker prevents calls to the failing downstream service to allow it time to recover and to protect caller resources.

slides.title: The Three Pillars of Observability
slides.content:
📊 Metrics, Logs, and Traces

Observability is not just monitoring. It's about asking your system 'Why is this happening?'. Logs tell you what happened. Metrics tell you how much of it happened. Traces show you how a request traveled through multiple services.

box.title: The Global Trace ID
box.content:
🕵️ Context Propagation

By injecting a traceparent header into every request (HTTP or Message), you can stitch together a single operation as it moves from Gateway ──► Order Service ──► Inventory ──► Payment. Jaeger or Tempo are best for visualization.

slides.title: Distributed Trace Visualization
slides.content:
Order Request [TraceID: 154a2]
│
├─► [Gateway] ..................... 50ms
│    └─► [User Auth] .............. 20ms
│
├─► [Order Service] ............... 200ms
│    ├─► [Inventory Check] ........ 40ms
│    └─► [RabbitMQ Publish] ....... 10ms
│
└──► [Payment Service] (Async) .... 150ms

note.text: Traces help identify exactly which microservice is causing a performance bottleneck.

box.title: Terminus & K8s Probes
box.content:
🏥 Self-Healing Readiness

A simple 'status 200' is not enough. You need Liveness Probes (is the app crashed?) and Readiness Probes (can it handle traffic?). NestJS Terminus allows you to check DB connectivity, memory usage, and third-party API availability dynamically.

slides.question: What is the primary purpose of a Distributed Trace in a Microservices architecture?
options: To store user passwords across services
To track the end-to-end path of a single request across multiple services
To measure the CPU usage of a physical server
slides.explanation: Distributed tracing is essential for debugging performance issues and errors in complex request flows across service boundaries.

slides.title: 8. Fortress Architecture
slides.subtitle: NestJS as an Intelligent API Gateway.

box.title: The Entry Point
box.content:
🛡️ Defense in Depth

An API Gateway handles unified Authentication, Authorization, Rate Limiting, and Request Transformation. In 2026, we also use it for Protocol Translation (e.g., converting HTTP requests to internal gRPC calls).

slides.title: Modern Authentication (OAuth2/JWT)
slides.content:
🔐 Stateless Security

Using @nestjs/passport and @nestjs/jwt, we implement stateless authentication. The Gateway validates the JWT signatures from providers like Keycloak or Auth0 and propagates the 'User Identity' to internal services via headers or Protobuf metadata.
slides.caption: Gateway-level Authorization

box.title: Advanced Access Control
box.content:
🎭 granular Permissions

Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is simple: 'Admin' can do anything. Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) is : 'Managers' can only view orders 'from their department' during 'business hours'. Use custom Nest Guards to implement ABAC logic.

slides.title: RBAC vs ABAC Comparison
rightPoints: ✅ No need for new roles for exceptions.

box.title: Small, Secure Images
box.code:
FROM node:23-alpine AS build
...
FROM node:23-alpine AS runtime
COPY --from=build /app/dist ./dist
CMD ["node", "dist/main"]
box.content: Don't ship your dev dependencies or source code. Multi-stage builds create images that are 80% smaller and significantly more secure by isolating the runtime environment.

slides.title: Kubernetes: The Operating System for Microservices
slides.content:
☸️ Orchestration Reality

Kubernetes handles Service Discovery, Load Balancing, Scaling, and Self-Healing. In an '' setup, use Horizontal Pod Autoscaling based on custom metrics (like RabbitMQ queue length) rather than just CPU usage.

box.title: Infrastructure as Code
box.content:
🚢 Deploying with Confidence

Use Helm to package your NestJS applications as versioned charts. Combine with ArgoCD for a GitOps workflow: your Git repo is the single source of truth for the cluster state. Simply push code to master, and K8s syncs automatically.

slides.title: Production CI/CD Workflow
slides.content:
Code Push ──► GitHub Actions ──► Unit Tests & Lint
                                     │
                                     ▼
  K8s Deployment ◄── ArgoCD ◄── Build Docker Image
       │                             │
       ▼                             ▼
  Monitoring  ◄── Prometheus ◄── Docker Registry

note.text: The transition from code to production should be entirely automated.

slides.title: 10. Mastering the Machine
slides.subtitle: Dynamic Modules, Scopes, and circular Dependencies.

box.title: Reclaim Module Flexibility
box.content:
🏗️ Universal Configurable Modules

Dynamic modules allow you to export modules that can be configured by the importing module. This is the pattern behind TypeOrmModule.forRoot(). It enables creating highly reusable library modules.

box.title: forwardRef() to the Rescue
box.content: When Service A depends on Service B, and Service B depends on Service A, the DI container fails. Use forwardRef() to break the cycle. However, extensive use of this pattern suggests your modules are too tightly coupled.

slides.title: Request Lifecycle Mastery
slides.content:
🔄 The Pipeline Order

Understanding the exact execution order is critical for debugging: Middleware ──► Guards ──► Interceptors (Pre) ──► Pipes ──► Handler ──► Interceptors (Post) ──► Filters. Mastering this flow allows you to place logic precisely where it belongs.
slides.caption: NestJS Request Context

box.title: Keep Shipping
box.content:
Architecture is not about making 'perfect' decisions. It's about making 'reversible' decisions and managing trade-offs. The systems you build today with NestJS will be the foundation of tomorrow's enterprise scale.

🚀 Go Build Something Great!

box.title: The Floor is Yours
box.content:
💬 Any Questions?

Scan the code to join our community or reach out for architecture reviews.
